signals.title: Duval County schedules Cazadores Solar tax-abatement hearing and consideration
signals.summary: Duval County published notice of a public hearing and possible approval of a Cazadores Solar tax-abatement agreement after creation of the project zone; no final agreement outcome is supplied.
signals.quote:
The Commissioners’ Court of Duval County, Texas (the "County"), having created the
Cazadores Solar LLC Tax Abatement Reinvestment Zone in Duval County, Texas, will consider
entering into a tax abatement agreement, as follows:

 1. The names of the property owners for the location of the following projects
 Cazadores Solar LLC are as follows:

 Property
 ID Name of Property Owner Location

 15672 Broze, Clarence Jr. LTS 4 BLK 3 R S C F & G T

 15702 Cantu, Homero & Norma LTS N/2 OF 1,ES/4 OF 1 BLK 10 R S C F T & G T

 15705 Schindler Brothers LTS 2 BLK 10 R S C F & G T

 15699 T&K Ranches LLC LTS 1 THRU 4 BLK 9 R S C F & G T

 15707 Guerra, Diana LTS 1/2 BLK 11 R S C F & G T

 15710 The Schindler Bro LTS 4 BLK 11 R S C F & G T

 15709 The Schindler Bro LTS 3 BLK 11 R S C F & G T

 15720 Broze, Clarence Jr. & Janet LTS N/PT OF 1/2 BLK 15 R S C F & G T

 15719 Broze, Clarence Jr. & Janet LTS N/PT OF 1/2 BLK 15 R S C F & G T

 15718 Broze, Clarence Jr. & Janet S/PT OF 1/2 & N/PT OF 3/4 BLK 15 R S C F &
 G T

 15721 Broze, Clarence Jr. & Janet LTS MID/PT of 3/4 BLK 15 R S C F & G T

 15722 Broze, Clarence Jr. & Janet LTS S/PT OF 3,4 BLK 15 R S C F & G T

 2. The applicants for the tax abatement agreement is Cazadores Solar LLC.

 3. The name of the reinvestment zone has been established by Duval County as the
 Cazadores Solar LLC Tax Abatement Reinvestment Zone Number 1, and is located Duval
 County, Texas, and more particularly described above.

 1

 4. The improvements to be included in the tax abatement agreement generally consist of
 facilities to be used for the proposed generation of solar power, as well their support
 infrastructure.

 5. Proposed improvements would consist of PV solar modules, connected from strings which
 are subsequently connected in parallel and mounted on rows of horizontal, single axis
 trackers. The improvements will also feature central power inverters and transformers to
 convert DC power to AC electricity. In addition to the major equipment, there will be the
 supporting electrical collection system and supporting infrastructure facilities to be
 constructed and improved as necessary, as well as overhead transmission lines, a collection
 substation to permit the interconnection and transmission of electricity generated by the
 facility.

 6. The estimated costs of the improvements are approximately a total of $ 251
 Million for the solar power facility.

 7. Pursuant to the Texas Open Meetings Act (the “Act”), the County will consider
 approval of the tax abatement agreement in its regularly scheduled meeting on July 13,
 2026, at 9:00 a.m., at the Duval County Courthouse, in the Commissioners Courtroom. A
 public hearing to receive public comment on this tax abatement will also be noticed and will
 be held at 9:30 a.m.
signals.authorityCaveat: The record concerns possible project facilitation and does not establish final approval or a generally applicable local restriction.
signals.model: ChatGPT Pro extended review + deterministic local verification

signals.title: Duval County posts notice of proposed Cielo Vista Solar abatement
signals.summary: Duval County’s notice identifies Cielo Vista Solar as a tax-abatement applicant, describes proposed solar-power improvements, and states that the county will consider approval on July 13, 2026.
signals.quote:
2. The applicants for the tax abatement agreements are Cielo Vista Solar LLC, Cielo Vista
 Storage LLC, and Cielo Vista Power LLC.

 3. The name of the reinvestment zone has been established by Duval County as the Cielo
 Vista Solar LLC Tax Abatement Reinvestment Zone, and is located northeast of Duval County
 along Hwy 59 and south CR115. Duval County, Texas, and more particularly described above.

 4. The improvements to be included in the tax abatement agreements generally consist of
 facilities to be used for the proposed generation of solar power, battery storage and natural
 gas processing facilty as well their support infrastructure.

 5. Proposed improvements would consist of PV solar modules, connected from strings which
 are subsequently connected in parallel and mounted on rows of horizontal, single axis
 trackers. The improvements will also feature central power inverters and transformers to
 convert DC power to AC electricity. In addition to the major equipment, there will be the
 supporting electrical collection system and supporting infrastructure facilities to be
 constructed and improved as necessary, as well as overhead transmission lines, a collection
 substation to permit the interconnection and transmission of electricity generated by the
 facility. The proposed improvements for the Power facility include roads , fences, gates,
 underground electrical collection systems, and natural gas turbine generators, water and
 wastewater lines. Proposed improvements to battery storage facilty include battery energy
 storage systems, central power inverters and transformers, security fences and gates, energy
 collection sub station and related structures.

 6. The estimated costs of the improvements are approximately a total of $ 539
 Million for the natural gas power facility. For the solar power facility, the estimated costs
 are approximately $477 million and for the battery storage facility the estimated cost is
 approximatley $146.46 million .

 7. Pursuant to the Texas Open Meetings Act (the “Act”), the County will consider
 approval of the three tax abatement agreements in its regularly scheduled meeting on July
 13, 2026, at 9:00 a.m., at the Duval County Courthouse, in the Commissioners Courtroom.
signals.authorityCaveat: The record concerns possible project facilitation and does not establish final approval or a generally applicable local restriction.

signals.title: Deaf Smith County considers Google data-center tax abatements
signals.summary: Duplicate official agenda records place a tax-abatement agreement for Google or affiliates and multiple data-center phases before the Commissioners Court; no outcome is supplied.
signals.quote:
8. Discuss and consider approval of a Tax Abatement Agreement between Deaf Smith County
 and Google LLC or its affiliates authorizing up to eight property tax abatements.

 Information relating to this Tax Abatement Agreement as required by Section 312.207(c)
 and Section 312.208( a) of the Texas Tax Code is as follows:
• Name of Property Owners: Google LLC and/or affiliates, IP Roman Land, LLC
• Name of Applicant for Abatement: Google LLC and/or affiliates, IP Roman Land, LLC
• Name of Reinvestment Zone: Deaf Smith County Reinvestment Zone #2026-02
• Location of Google Reinvestment Zone #2026-02: Deaf Smith County Appraisal District
 Property ID 7139, being BLOCK K-7 SECTION 72, 648.5 AC, A-1055,
 Abstract/Subdivision AK07 (Geographic ID: AK07-72-3); Deaf Smith County Appraisal
 District Property ID 7142, being BLOCK K-7 SECTION 73, 653.3 AC, A-98,
 Abstract/Subdivision AK07 (Geographic ID: AK07-73-l); Deaf Smith County Appraisal
 District Property ID 7158, being BLOCK K-7 SECTION 88 ALL, 654 AC, A-1164,
 Abstract/Subdivision AK07 (Geographic ID: AK07-88-1); and Deaf Smith County
 Appraisal District Property ID 7159, being BLOCK K-7 SECTION 89, 649.5 AC, A-20,
 Abstract/Subdivision AK07 (Geographic ID: AK07-89-1).
• Estimated Cost of Improvements: $1,000,000,000 per data center or phase, with up to
 eight phases contemplated.
• Description and Nature of Improvements: Expected improvements consist of: (i) one or
 more (up to eight) data centers and related improvements; (ii) other buildings and ancillary
 facilities supporting the operation of the data center or centers; (iii) such additional related
 improvements, including, but not limited to, required parking, landscaping and all other
 improvements; and (iv) computer servers and other equipment related to the operation of
 the data center or centers.
signals.authorityCaveat: The record concerns possible project facilitation and does not establish final approval or a generally applicable local restriction.

signals.title: Wolf Spring Solar reinvestment zone adopted
signals.summary: After a public hearing, Motley County adopted the Wolf Spring Solar reinvestment-zone resolution.
signals.quote:
Item 9 on the agenda was Public Hearing on intention of Motley County to designate the Wolf Spring Solar
Reinvestment Zone. Establish the boundaries and thereof and provide for an effective date pursuant to Chapter 312 of
the Texas Tax Code. (No One present from Public for the hearing)

No. 623

Next on the agenda was to consider and act on Approval of Resolution designating the Wolf Spring Solar
Reinvestment Zone, establishing the boundaries thereof and providing for an effective date, pursuant to Chapter 312 of
the Texas Tax Code. Commissioner Campbell moved that the County approve and adopt the Resolution, as presented,
designating the Wolf Spring Solar Reinvestment Zone, establishing the boundaries thereof and providing for an effective
date, seconded by Commissioner Brooks. Motion passed with a 4 to 0 vote.
signals.authorityCaveat: The zone supports tax-abatement eligibility and does not restrict solar development.

signals.title: Gray County entered road-use and fire-safety agreement for data-center project
signals.summary: Gray County and IP Meitner entered an effective agreement covering county-road use, local plans, fire-safety training, and local-spending efforts for the planned data-center project.
signals.quote:
THIS ROAD USE, LOCAL PLANS, AND FIRE SAFETY TRAINING AGREEMENT
(the “Agreement”) is made and entered into by and between GRAY COUNTY, TEXAS (the
“County”), and IP Meitner, LLC, a Delaware Limited Liability Company (“Project Entity”),
effective as_Octobey 31, 2025 (the “Effective Date”). Capitalized terms used in this
Agreement and not otherwise defined herein shall have the same meaning ascribed to such terms
in the Tax Abatement Agreement (defined below).

RECITALS

WHEREAS, the County and Project Entity entered into that certain Tax Abatement
Agreement dated Cc tober 31, 2025 (the “Tax Abatement Agreement”); and

WHEREAS, the Project Entity intends to construct, equip, operate and maintain one or
more Data Centers and may construct, equip, operate, and maintain related ancillary buildings,
structures and infrastructure that support or are related to the data center operations, including but
not limited to, utility buildings, structures, and appurtenances within the Property (as defined
below) and the Reinvestment Zone (collectively, the “Project”); and

WHEREAS, the Project Entity and the County wish to memorialize certain agreements
regarding use of County Roads, fire safety and training, and local spending efforts relating to the
Project.
signals.authorityCaveat: This is project-specific facilitation and does not establish a generally applicable local restriction.
signals.model: ChatGPT Pro extended review + deterministic local verification

signals.title: Hansford County tax-abatement policy includes solar facilities
signals.summary: Hansford County’s adopted tax-abatement guidelines identify solar facilities as eligible facilities that may be considered for tax abatement.
signals.quote:
This policy is effective as of the date of adoption, and shall at all times be kept current
 with regard to the needs of the County and reflective of the official views of the County, and
 shall be reviewed every two (2) years.

Hansford County Tax Abatement Guidelines and Criteria Page 1 of9

The adoption of these guidelines and criteria by the Commissioners Court does not:

 1. limit the discretion of the governing body to decide whether to enter into a specific
 Tax Abatement Agreement;

 2. limit the discretion of the governing body to delegate to its employees the authority
 to determine whether or not the governing body should consider a particular
 application or request for Tax Abatement; or

 3. create any property, contract, or other legal right in any person to have the
 governing body consider or grant a specific application or request for Tax
 Abatement.

 SECTION I. DEFINITIONS

 A. "Abatement" or "Tax Abatement" means the full or partial exemption from ad
 valorem taxes of certain property in a reinvestment zone designated for economic development
 purposes.

 B. "Agreement" or "Tax Abatement Agreement" means a contractual Agreement
 between a property owner and/or lessee and the County.

 C. "Base Year Value" means the assessed value on the eligible property as of January
 1 preceding the execution of the Agreement.

 D. "Deferred Maintenance" means improvements necessary for continued operation
 which do not improve productivity or alter the process technology.

 E. "Eligible Facilities" means new, expanded, or modernized buildings and
 structures, including fixed machinery and equipment, which is reasonably likely as a result of
 granting the Abatement to contribute to the retention or expansion of primary employment or to
 attract major investment in the reinvestment zone that would be a benefit to the property and that
 would contribute to the economic development of the County, but does not include facilities which
 are intended to be primarily to provide goods or services to residents for existing businesses located
 in the County such as, but not limited to, restaurants and retail sales establishments. Eligible
 facilities may include, but shall not be limited to:

 aqua culturelagriculture facilities regional entertainment/tourism facilities
 distribution center facilities research service facilities
 manufacturing facilities regional service facilities
 office buildings historic buildings in a designated area
 other basic industrial facilities wind, solar, or other renewable energy
 facilities
signals.authorityCaveat: The guidelines establish eligibility for consideration only; they do not guarantee or approve any project-specific abatement.
signals.model: ChatGPT Pro extended review + deterministic local verification

signals.title: Knox tax-abatement guidelines include data centers
signals.summary: The county's adopted guidelines list computer and data centers as facilities eligible to seek discretionary tax abatement.
signals.quote:
The adoption of these guidelines and criteria by the Commissioners Court does not:

1. limit the discretion of the governing body to decide whether to enter into a specific
Tax Abatement Agreement;

2. limit the discretion of the governing body to delegate to its employees the authority
to determine whether or not the governing body should consider a particular
application or request for Tax Abatement; or

3. create any property, contract, or other legal right in any person to have the
governing body consider or grant a specific application or request for Tax
Abatement.

SECTION I. DEFINITIONS

A. "Abatement" or “Tax Abatement” means the full or partial exemption from ad
valorem taxes of certain property in a reinvestment zone designated for economic development
purposes.

B. "Agreement" or “Tax Abatement Agreement” means a contractual Agreement
between a property owner and/or lessee and the County.

Cc. "Base Year Value" means the assessed value on the eligible property as of January
1 preceding the execution of the Agreement.

D. "Deferred Maintenance" means improvements necessary for continued operation
which do not improve productivity or alter the process technology.

E. “Eligible Facilities" means new, expanded, or modernized buildings and
structures, including fixed machinery and equipment, which is reasonably likely as a result of
granting the Abatement to contribute to the retention or expansion of primary employment or to
attract major investment in the reinvestment zone that would be a benefit to the property and that
would contribute to the economic development of the County, but does not include facilities which
are intended to be primarily to provide goods or services to residents for existing businesses located
in the County such as, but not limited to, restaurants and retail sales establishments. Eligible
facilities may include, but shall not be limited to:

aquaculture/agriculture facilities
distribution center facilities
manufacturing facilities

office buildings

other basic industrial facilities

regional entertainment/tourism facilities
research service facilities

regional service facilities

historic buildings in a designated area

Knox County Tax Abatement Guidelines and Criteria (2024) Page 2 of 9

computer/data center
wind, solar, or other renewable energy facilities
signals.authorityCaveat: Eligibility creates no right to an abatement and does not regulate or approve a specific project.
signals.model: ChatGPT Pro extended review + deterministic local verification

signals.title: Tom Green guidelines include solar facilities
signals.summary: County tax-abatement guidelines list photovoltaic solar facilities among covered renewable-energy facility types.
signals.quote:
Renewable Energy Facility” means buildings and structures, including but not
limited to electricity generating equipment (such as wind turbines or photovoltaic
solar panels), electric transmission lines, electric power substations, electrical
gathering equipment, communications systems and roads, fixed machinery and
equipment, used or to be used to provide electrical energy, and which meet the
definition of “Renewable Energy Electric Generation” in Chapter 313 of the Texas
Tax Code.

Guidelines & Criteria — Page 14

6-Step Abatement Process

Tax Code Chapter 312 governs reinvestment zones and tax abatements. This chapter enables
property taxing entities, excluding school districts, to curb the property taxes assessed on
tangible personal property or real property due to the improvements or repairs to the property.
Only the property located within a reinvestment zone qualifies for a tax abatement agreement.
Hence, a tax abatement agreement is an agreement limiting the increase in the value of the
property taxes due to improvements or repairs to real property. Such agreements are limited to
ten (10) years.
signals.authorityCaveat: The excerpt establishes eligibility language but not the adoption date, a specific award, or any restriction.

signals.title: Atascosa County monitors reported data center proposal
signals.summary: Atascosa County stated that it had not been contacted by the company and was monitoring reports and gathering information about a potential data center project; no application or decision is reported.
signals.quote:
JOURDANTON, TEXAS — Atascosa County has received numerous questions regarding
reports of a potential data center project that may be located within the county.

At this time, Atascosa County has not been contacted by the company associated with
the proposed project and has not had direct discussions regarding the development.
Because of that, County officials are unable to confirm many of the details currently
being circulated.

Like many residents, County officials have become aware of information circulating
online and throughout the community. We understand there are questions regarding
what this project could mean for Atascosa County and what, if any, role the County may
have moving forward.

The County is paying close attention to developments and will continue working to
gather information as it becomes available.
signals.authorityCaveat: The county expressly said it had no direct contact with the company and could not confirm circulated project details.

signals.title: Hornet Solar abatement amendment noticed
signals.summary: The county issued notice of possible action on a first amendment to the Hornet Solar tax-abatement agreement.
signals.quote:
PURPOSE: The Swisher County Commissioners Court will discuss and take possible action regarding
a First Amendment to the Tax Abatement Agreement with Hornet Solar LLC, pursuant to Chapter 312
of the Texas Tax Code.

Property Owner and Applicant for Tax Abatement: Hornet Solar LLC
Proposed Reinvestment Zone: Hornet Solar LLC Reinvestment Zone

Property in Proposed Reinvestment Zone: See attached map and legal description provided in
Exhibit “A” attached hereto and incorporated herein for all purposes.

Anticipated Improvements: The general description of the nature of the improvements included in
the agreement is as follows:

Hornet Solar LLC anticipates constructing a solar power facility with a roughly 310 megawatts
(AC) of Nameplate Capacity located in the Reinvestment Zone. Improvements may include
(but are not limited to) approximately 1,568,000 solar modules/panels; approximately 164
inverters; metal mounting system with tracking capabilities; battery or battery system;
underground conduit; communications cables and electric system wiring; combiner boxes;
project substation including breakers; transformer and meters; overhead transmission lines;
operations and maintenance facility; fencing: telephone and internet communication system,
and meteorological equipment to measure solar irradiation and weather conditions.

Summary of Proposed Amendments: The First Amendment to the Tax Abatement Agreement would
increase the megawatts from 310 (AC) of Nameplate Capacity to 600 (AC) of Nameplate Capacity and
extend the Commercial Operations date so that the Owner agrees that the Project shall achieve
Commercial Operations on or before December 31, 2025.
signals.authorityCaveat: The notice does not establish final approval and is separate from the earlier Hornet Solar II record.

signals.title: Caldwell County adopts resolution seeking statewide data-center safeguards
signals.summary: Caldwell County adopted a resolution supporting responsibly designed and sited data centers while opposing high-volume potable-water cooling without safeguards and urging state reporting, impact analysis, sustainability standards, and greater local land-use and infrastructure authority.
signals.quote:
NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED BY THE CALDWELL COUNTY COMMISSIONERS COURT
THAT:

SECTION 1. POSITION OF THE COURT

The Caldwell County Commissioners Courl supports responsible economic development, including appropriately
 designed and sited data center facilities. However, the Court formally expresses opposition to the use of open-loop
 evaporative cooling systems or other high-volume potable water consumption technologies in large-scale, high
 continuous-load data center facilities within water-constrained regions, absent demonstrated sustainable supply and
 mitigation safeguards. The Court further opposes any data center development that fails to incorporate adequate
 safeguards to protect counfy resources and residents.

The Court further states that suppoft for additional data center development in the State of Texas should be
conditioned upon the implementation of comprehensive, enforceable safeguards to:

 A. Protect electric grid reliability and prevent cost burdens on residential, agricultural, and small-business
 ratepayers;
 B. Ensure responsible, sustainable, and transparent water use practices:
 C. Protect agricultural lands, rangelands, wildlife habitats, and natural drainage systems by requiring careful
 site selection, avoidance of prime farmland and ecologically sensitive areas where practicable, and
 appropriate mitigation of habitat fragmentation and stormwater impacts;
 D. Prevent degradation of county roads, drainage systems, and other public infrastructure; and
 E. Safeguard community welfare, public health, and environmental quality.

SECTION 2. TRANSPARENCY AND REPORTING

The Court urges the State of Texas and relevant regulatory agencies to require full and transparent public reporling
of anticipated electricity demand, water usage, and infrastructure impacts for all proposed data center developments
prior to final approval.

SECTION 3. INDEPENDENT IMPACT ANALYSIS

No future data center project should proceed without a rigorous, independent assessment of:
 A. Impacts on ERCOT grid reliability and transmission capacity;
 B. Impacts on regional water availability and drought contingency planning;
 C. Impacts on agricultural lands, rangelands, wildlife habitats, natural drainage patterns, and potential habitat
 fragmentation, including effects on prime farmland, soil integrity, and stormwater runoff;
 D. Long-term infrastructure costs borne by taxpayers; and
 E. Effects on surrounding residential, agricultural, and commercial land uses.

SECTION 4. RESOURCE SUSTAINABILITY STANDARDS

The Court encourages adoption of statewide planning and regulatory standards prioritizing sustainability, including:
 A. Integration of water-efficient or closed-loop cooling technologies;
 B. Utilization of reclaimed, recycled, or non-potable water sources where feasible;
 C. Demand response participation and load management requirements to protect grid stability.

SECTION 5. LEGISLATIVE ACTION REQUESTED

The Caldwell County Commissioners Court respectfully requests that the Governor and Texas Legislature:

1. Designate this matter as an interim study priority for appropriate legislative committees to evaluate grid
reliability, r,vater consumption, local authority, infrastructure impacts, and fiscal implications; and
2. Consider, if circumstances warrant timely action, inclusion of this issue on the call of a Special Session of

the Texas Legislature to address urgent reliability, water supply, and local governance concerns; and
3. Enact legislation providing counties and municipalities meaningful participation and authority in land use,
infrastructure planning, and resource impact review related to large-scale data center developments.

PASSED, APPROVED, AND RESOLVED this the l4th day of May, 2026
signals.authorityCaveat: This action addresses state policy and does not itself impose a county restriction.
signals.model: ChatGPT Pro extended review + deterministic local verification

signals.title: Caldwell presentation summarizes limits on county data-center authority
signals.summary: The county presentation states that counties lack zoning power, may not enact a moratorium, and must approve compliant permit applications within 30 days.
signals.quote:
Regulation of Data Centers

County Power to Regulate Development

• Under Texas law, Counties have very little authority to regulate
 development.

 • Counties do not have zoning or similar land use controls. There is
 no vote on allowing or disallowing a use to occur.

 • Counties can regulate the subdivision of land but must approve the
 plat if it complies with regulations. Must act within 30 days of
 submittal.

 • Counties may require a development permit (primarily limited to
 drainage review) but must approve the permit if it complies with
 regulations. Must act within 30 days of submittal.

County Power to Regulate Development

• Caldwell County is not a water provider and has no approval role.
 • Decisions regarding the available capacity and the provision of
 water are made by the water provider. Examples of water providers:
 • Maxwell Water Supply
 • Goforth Water Supply
 • Countyline Water Supply
 • Tri-County Water Supply

• Caldwell County is not an electrical power provider and has no
 approval role. Power providers are:
 • Lower Colorado River Authority (LCRA) – Wholesale power supplier
 • Bluebonnet Electric Coop – Retail power supplier

County Power to Regulate Development

• County may not enact a moratorium.

• The County cannot pause or stop development.

• County must approve all permit applications within
 30 days if application complies with regulations.
signals.authorityCaveat: This reflects an existing policy or authority statement, not a newly adopted restriction.

signals.title: Caldwell County outlines negotiated data-center development strategy
signals.summary: The county strategy proposes development agreements, possible Chapter 312 and PILOT terms, closed-loop non-potable cooling, environmental safeguards, and roadway commitments for data centers.
signals.quote:
The Caldwell County Data Center Strategy

• Negotiate a Development Agreement that allows for a give and take
 discussion. Examples of Development Agreement provisions include:
 • Potential 312 agreement to keep value off tax roll subject to pilot
 payments to the County in lieu of taxation. This does not impact tax
 rate.

 • Require closed loop, non-potable water, cooling system. Daily
 water use is equal to only bathroom and kitchen usage levels.

 • Require stormwater water quality water quality treatment with
 specified TSS removal. (TCEQ-RG348 Edwards Aquifer Rules –
 Technical Guidance for Best Management Practices)

The Caldwell County Data Center Strategy

• Require 80% impervious cover limit.

• Require Dark Sky provisions.

• Require 2D modeling of floodplains for more accurate modeling and permitting.

• Require onsite natural gas power generation for cleaner emissions.

• Require highest level of TCEQ wastewater treatment at 5-5-2-0.5 should a discharge
 permit be requested.

• Obtain cost free dedication of future roadway ROWs and funds for roadway
 construction.

Conclusion

• This development strategy results in a better project for the
 citizens of Caldwell County than could otherwise happen
 with no input from the County.
signals.authorityCaveat: The record concerns possible project facilitation and does not establish final approval or a generally applicable local restriction.

signals.title: Duval County publishes proposed solar tax-abatement criteria
signals.summary: Duval County Commissioners Court unanimously authorized proposed amendments and approved publication of added evaluation, reporting, compliance, fee, and enforcement provisions for solar projects under its tax-abatement framework; final adoption is not shown.
signals.quote:
NOTICE IS HEREBY GIVEN that the Duval County Commissioners Court has unanimously
authorized proposed amendments to the existing 2024 Duval County Tax Abatement
Policies and Guidelines adopted pursuant to Chapter 312 of the Texas Tax Code on June 8th
2026.

The purpose of these amendments is to supplement and enhance the County's existing tax
abatement framework by establishing additional standards and requirements applicable to
future tax abatement agreements considered by Duval County. The additional provisions to
the previous 2024 Duval County Tax Abatment Policies and Guidelines can be summarized
as follows. These new guidelines are posted on the Duval County website.

1. COMMUNITY BENEFITS AGREEMENTS

Applicants seeking tax abatements may be required to enter into Community Benefits
Agreements that provide measurable economic and community benefits to Duval County
residents and businesses.

2. LOCAL ECONOMIC PARTICIPATION

The Commissioners Court may consider an applicant's commitment to utilizing local
contractors, suppliers, service providers, and workforce resources during the construction
and operational phases of a project.

3. CLARIFICATION OF PARTICIPATING TAXING ENTITIES

Any tax abatement granted by Duval County applies solely to County ad valorem taxes
unless separately approved by another taxing entity.

4. RENEWABLE ENERGY PROJECTS

Additional evaluation criteria are established for solar, wind, battery storage, and other
renewable energy projects.

5. APPLICATION, REPORTING, AND COMPLIANCE REQUIREMENTS

Additional guidance is provided regarding application procedures, annual reporting

obligations, compliance verification, monitoring requirements, administrative fees, and
enforcement mechanisms.

6. MAXIMUM TERM AND RECAPTURE PROVISIONS

All tax abatement agreements remain subject to the requirements and limitations of
Chapter 312, Texas Tax Code.

APPROVED FOR PUBLICATION BY UNANIMOUS ACTION OF THE DUVAL COUNTY
COMMISSIONERS COURT on June 8th , 2026. A copy of the Commissioner’s Court Resolution
will be sent in the next week.
signals.authorityCaveat: Publication of proposed incentive-policy amendments does not establish final adoption or a general land-use restriction.

signals.title: Duval draft conditions data-center abatements on development agreements
signals.summary: Draft language to be added to county policy would bar approval of a data-center tax abatement unless the applicant and county execute a development agreement addressing environmental, safety, noise, water, infrastructure, monitoring, remedies, and public input.
signals.quote:
Development Agreements for Data Centers and Crypto-Mining Projects
(to be added to Section 5)

Data Centers and/or Crypto-Mining projects may collectively be referred to as a “Data
Center” project. No tax abatement shall be approved for a Data Center unless the
Applicant and the County enter into a written Development Agreement addressing the
matters set forth below.

 (a) Scope of Agreement. The Development Agreement shall, at a minimum, address:

 • Environmental protections: required permits and compliance with federal, state,
 and local environmental laws;
 • measures to prevent contamination of soil, groundwater, and surface water;
 • management and disposal of hazardous materials and waste; stormwater control
 and spill response plans;
 • Public health and safety: plans for chemical storage and handling, emergency
 response and evacuation procedures, coordination with local emergency services,
 and any public health monitoring required by the County;
 • Noise and vibration: baseline noise studies, design and operational requirements
 to control noise and vibration, maximum permitted sound levels at property lines,
 and mitigation measures (e.g., equipment enclosures, setbacks, vibration
 isolation);
 • Light and glare: exterior lighting plans that minimize off-site light trespass and
 glare (including shielded fixtures, cut-off angles, and timing controls);
 • compliance with County dark-sky or lighting ordinances where applicable;
 • Nuisance prevention: protocols to prevent odor, dust, electromagnetic
 interference, traffic impacts, and other deleterious effects on neighboring
 properties;
 • hours of operation, access controls, and landscaping/screening requirements;
 • Air emissions and thermal impacts: evaluation and mitigation of emissions and
 heat discharge (including HVAC and exhaust systems), and compliance with
 applicable air quality permits and standards;
 • Waste heat/use optimization: where feasible, consideration of beneficial reuse of
 waste heat (e.g., district heating or industrial reuse) and reporting on efforts to
 implement such measures;
 • Utilities and infrastructure impacts: assessment and mitigation of impacts on
 electric, water, wastewater, and telecommunications infrastructure; requirement
 for coordination with utility providers and for obtaining necessary capacity
 assurances;
 • Monitoring, reporting and audits: baseline studies, periodic monitoring, public
 reporting requirements, and County audit or inspection rights to verify
 compliance (subject to confidentiality protections for proprietary information);
 • Remedial action and corrective measures: standards and timelines for addressing
 violations, including required remediation, suspension or modification of
 operations, and financial assurance or performance security where warranted;

 • Remedies and enforcement: civil penalties, suspension or revocation of abatement
 benefits, indemnification obligations, and other remedies available to the County
 for material noncompliance; and
 • Term, transfer and assignment: provisions governing the duration of obligations,
 treatment of assignments or transfers of project ownership, and obligations that
 survive sale or termination.

(b) Standards and Flexibility. The Development Agreement shall require compliance with
objective standards (e.g., numeric noise limits, permit conditions), but may allow
reasonable operational flexibility where consistent with protecting public health, safety,
and the environment. The County may adopt technical standards or model provisions by
reference.

(c) Public Input. The County shall provide for public notice and a reasonable opportunity
for comment on each proposed Development Agreement for each Data Center project.
The County may require public hearings prior to approval.
signals.authorityCaveat: The language is a draft condition on discretionary tax abatements and development agreements; final adoption and any broader regulatory authority are not shown.
signals.model: ChatGPT Pro extended review + deterministic local verification

signals.title: Ector County posts quorum notice for a data-center informational meeting
signals.summary: Ector County noticed that Commissioners Court members might attend a Chamber-hosted data-center informational meeting and expressly stated that no court business or deliberations would occur.
signals.quote:
NOTICE OF POSSIBLE QUORUM
 Ector County Commissioners' Court

 Data Center Informational Meeting
 Hosted by:

signals.title: Wheeler County judge calls for state data-center safeguards
signals.summary: The county judge discussed AI data centers and urged state-level safeguards while acknowledging both potential benefits and concerns.
signals.quote:
I would like to begin with some information I researched regarding questions that I have been
asked related to AI data centers. We all have questions and that includes me. I have diligently been
attempting to educate myself about the rapidly growing AI data center industry and have
researched multiple companies in the industry.

The first questions on most people’s minds are, what are these data centers and what do they
contribute to our world? According to IBM’s Alexandra Jonker and Alice Gomstyn, an AI data
center is a facility that houses the IT infrastructure needed to train, deploy and deliver artificial
intelligence applications and services.1 Basically, these facilities house ultra-fast supercomputers.
They process petabytes (1,024 terabytes or 1 quadrillion bytes) of raw data that train complex
machine learning. Whether you intend to or not, you are utilizing these services on a daily basis
and are contributing to the demand for the rapid growth of AI data centers. Our phones, cars, TV’s,
office software systems, security systems, etc. all need the services of these supercomputers in
order to work properly.

I have very mixed thoughts about the pros and cons of this industry. While I would like to have
the time and be able to see more long-term evidence of the effects of this industry on our
communities, the pace at which its growth is occurring may not allow us to experience that before
it is already a part of our County. Hopefully, the Texas State Legislature will step in at some point
(sooner rather than later) and put safeguards and/or regulations in place in order to give everyone
a chance to “catch up” and determine if it is something we are willing to support. A similar situation
occurred when wind energy was introduced into the Texas Panhandle. It was new and no one truly
knew what to expect. Regulations were non-existent and had to be put in place. This takes time.
Time is something we don’t seem to have a lot of right now.
signals.authorityCaveat: The statement is state-policy advocacy by the county judge, not adoption of a local restriction.
signals.model: ChatGPT Pro extended review + deterministic local verification

signals.title: Houston County adopts resolution urging state pause and data-center standards
signals.summary: The Commissioners Court voted 5-0 to request that state officials pause pending data-center projects and adopt uniform standards addressing water, power, roads, and drainage.
signals.quote:
Commissioner Henderson moved to adopt a
 Resolution to request State Officials to pause any pending data center projects and adopt
 comprehensive, uniform state standards for data centers to ensure water supplies, power
 grids, road and drainage systems can be reviewed and protected. Commissioner Stokes
 seconded. Motion carried 5 to 0.
signals.authorityCaveat: This action addresses state policy and does not itself impose a county restriction.

signals.title: Houston County approves state petition on data-center safeguards
signals.summary: The Commissioners Court voted 5-0 to sign a petition asking state officials to consider fire equipment, environmental mitigation, permitting, roads, and drainage for data centers.
signals.quote:
Commissioner Stokes moved to sign a
 Petition for Data Center considerations including fire department equipment, environmental
 mitigations, required permitting in unincorporated areas, road planning and cost as well as
 drainage engineering for non-subdivision/unplatted developments to be submitted to the
 State of Texas Governor and other appropriate elected officials and associated State
 Department Heads. Commissioner Henderson seconded. Motion carried 5 to 0.
signals.authorityCaveat: This action addresses state policy and does not itself impose a county restriction.

signals.title: Ellis County considers state-authority resolution for data centers
signals.summary: An agenda and transcript show Ellis County considering a resolution asking the state for enhanced data-center regulation and added county authority; a later transcript references the prior resolution, but the supplied records do not establish its disposition.
signals.quote:
[3.4 Discussion, consideration, and action of a Resolution by Ellis County Commissioners Court supporting enhanced regulation of data centers in the state of Texas. - County Commissioner Precinct 3 Louis Ponder ]
AND I WOULD FIRST ASK, UH, COMMISSIONER LEWIS PONDER TO DISCUSS THE ITEM.
OKAY.
ALRIGHT.
SO, UM, I GUESS I'LL EXPLAIN A LITTLE BIT FIRST BEFORE WE READ IT.
SO, WITH, UH, WITH THE EXPANSION OF DEVELOPMENT AND, AND IN ELLIS COUNTY OUTSIDE OF CITY LIMITS AND THINGS SUCH AS THAT, CURRENTLY THE, THE COMMISSIONER'S COURT, WE DON'T HAVE THE AUTHORITY TO REALLY REGULATE THESE THINGS, UH, MUCH.
I MEAN, DEVELOPMENT, HOUSING DEVELOPMENT, WE'VE GOT A MINIMAL THING WE CAN DO AS FAR AS LOT SIZE AND, AND, AND ROAD FRONTAGE AND STUFF LIKE THAT.
UH, DATA CENTERS, WE DON'T REALLY HAVE MUCH OF ANYTHING THAT WE CAN AFFECT.
I MEAN, IF THEY ASK FOR SOME ABATEMENT, WE CAN SAY, WELL, IF YOU WANT YOUR ABATEMENT, YOU GOTTA DO THESE THINGS.
BUT THAT'S ABOUT IT.
THAT'S REALLY ALL WE REALLY HAVE.
AND I THINK BECAUSE WE ARE GETTING SO MUCH MORE DEVELOPMENT NOW OUTSIDE THE CITY LIMITS, THE CITIES BEFORE WE'RE ABLE TO, TO REGULATE.
AND, AND THAT'S GENERALLY WHERE DEVELOPMENT GRAVITATED WAS TO CITY LIMITS.
NOW IT'S GRAVITATING OUTSIDE THE CITY LIMITS.
AND SO I, I FEEL IT IS TIME THAT THE COMMISSIONERS, AND I WANT THIS TO BE VERY LIMITED AUTHORITY, UH, BUT THE COMMISSIONER'S COURT NEEDS TO HAVE SOME AUTHORITY TO SAY YES, BUT OR NO BECAUSE, OR IF SO, DO THIS, DO THAT.
IT NEEDS TO BE LIMITED.
WHAT I DON'T WANT TO DO IS CREATE A SITUATION WHERE, AND I KNOW NONE OF MY COLLEAGUES HERE SITTING AT THESE TABLES WILL EVER OVERUSE OR ABUSE ANY AUTHORITY WERE GIVEN, BUT WE DON'T KNOW WHAT KIND OF COURT WE'LL HAVE IN FIVE YEARS, 10 YEARS, 20 YEARS DOWN THE ROAD.
YOU COULD END UP WITH A COURT THAT WANTS TO, YOU KNOW, ESSENTIALLY TREAT YOU LIKE SUBJECTS INSTEAD OF CONSTITUENTS.
AND, UM, SO WE NEED TO KEEP IT LIMITED.
UH, BUT WE DO NEED TO HAVE SOME CONTROL, SOME AUTHORITY TO WHERE WE CAN, WE CAN, WE CAN SAY YES, OR WE CAN SAY, WELL, IF YOU WANT TO, YOU CAN DO IT, BUT YOU'VE GOTTA MEET SPECIFIC CRITERIA AND THE, THE RESOLUTION GOES OVER PRETTY MUCH MOST OF THAT.
SO I'M, I'M, THIS IS NOT ABOUT BANNING DATA CENTERS MORATORIUMS ON DATA CENTERS.
THIS IS ABOUT HAVING SOME CONTROL OVER WHAT HAPPENS INSIDE OUR COUNTY, OUTSIDE OF CITY LIMITS.
YOU KNOW, ANYTHING INSIDE OF CITY LIMITS.
WE LET THE CITIES HAVE THAT WE STAY OUT OF THEIR WAY, THEY STAY OUT OF OUR LANE.
UH, AND WITH THAT, UM, COMMISSIONER LET 'EM LET 'EM KNOW WHERE WE GET THAT WE, WHERE WE GET THAT, OH YEAH, WE GET THAT AUTHORITY FROM THE LEGISLATORS, WHICH WE DON'T HAVE RIGHT NOW.
SO IT'S IMPORTANT THAT EVERYONE HERE THAT, THAT WANTS US TO BE GRANTED THAT TYPE OF AUTHORITY.
REACH OUT TO YOUR LEGISLATURES, REACH OUT TO YOUR FAMILY MEMBERS IN THE OTHER 254 COUNTIES IN TEXAS AND HAVE THEM REACH OUT TO THEIR LEGISLATORS BECAUSE IT'S GONNA TAKE A, IT'S GONNA TAKE A STRONG, UH, PUSH TO GET THE LEGISLATORS TO GIVE US THAT AUTHORITY.
AND AGAIN, I DON'T WANT TOO MUCH, I DON'T WANT TO CREATE A SITUATION WHERE YOU, YOU KNOW, YOU FOLKS CAN BE ABUSED, BUT I DO WANT TO BE
[00:10:01]
CORRECT.
YEAH.
VERY, VERY GOOD.
VERY GOOD.
AND, UH, THAT WAS A GREAT, UH, SUMMARY, COMMISSIONER PONDER.
AND THE RESOLUTION ESSENTIALLY RECOGNIZES THE STRAIN THAT DATA CENTERS, UH, PUT ON BOTH THE ELECTRIC GRID, ELECTRIC CONSUMPTION, WATER CONSUMPTION, UH, AS WELL AS POSSIBLE NOISE POLLUTION, AND OF COURSE, UH, LAND USE, YOU KNOW, AND THE, AND THE CHANGING CHARACTER OF AG LAND THAT, UH, YOU KNOW, SHOULD POSSIBLY BE PRESERVED FOR OTHER USES.
WHEREAS THESE, THESE DATA CENTERS SHOULD BE PERHAPS LOCATED IN AREAS OF THE STATE THAT DO NOT HAVE AS MUCH AGRICULTURAL USE.
SO THE, THE RESOLUTION RECOGNIZES THOSE THINGS, AND AS COMMISSIONER PONDER MENTIONED, UH, ASKS THE LEGISLATURE TO STUDY THIS ISSUE AND PASS APPROPRIATE LEGISLATION, WHICH MIGHT INCLUDE GRANTING ADDITIONAL REGULATORY AUTHORITY TO COUNTY GOVERNMENTS.
SO WITH THAT, WE DO HAVE A NUMBER OF PEOPLE THAT HAVE SIGNED UP TO, TO SPEAK.

signals.title: Cooke County adopts resolution seeking enhanced state data-center regulation
signals.summary: Cooke County adopted a resolution supporting responsible data-center development while urging stronger statewide safeguards and added local authority.
signals.quote:
SECTIO 1. POSITION OF THE COURT

The Commissioners Court supports responsible economic development, including data center facilities.
However, the Court formally expresses opposition to the use of open-loop evaporative cooling systems
or other high-volume potable water consumption technologies in large-scale data center faci lities within
Cooke County and other water-constrained regions. The Court further opposes any data center
development that fails to incorporate adequate safeguards to protect County resources and existing
residents. The Court further states that support for additional data center development in the State of
Texas should be conditioned upon the implementation of comprehensive, enforceable safeguards to:

• Protect electric grid reliability and prevent cost burdens on residential, agricultural, and small
 business ratepayers;
• Ensure responsible, sustainable, and transparent water use practices;
• Protect agricultural lands, rangelands, wildlife habitats, and natural drainage systems by requiring
 careful site selection, avoidance of prime farm land and ecologically sensitive areas where practicable,
 and appropriate mitigation of habitat fragmentation and storm water impacts;
• Prevent degradation of county roads, drainage systems, and other public infrastructure; and
• Safeguard community welfare, public health, and environmental quality.

SECTIO 2. TRANSPARENCY AND REPORT! G

The Court urges the State of Texas and relevant regulatory agencies to require full and transparent
public reporting of anticipated electricity demand, water usage, and infrastructure impacts for all
proposed data center developments prior to final approval.

SECTION 3. INDEPENDENT IMPACT ANALYSIS

No future data center project should proceed without a rigorous, independent assessment of:

 Impacts on ERCOT grid reliability and transmission capacity;
 Impacts on regional water avai lability and drought contingency planning;
 Impacts on agricultural lands, rangelands, wi ldlife habitats, natural drainage patterns, and potential
 habitat fragmentation, including effects on prime farmland, soil integrity, and stormwater runoff;
 Long-term infrastructure costs borne by taxpayers; and
 Effects on surrounding residential, agricultural, and commercial land uses.

SECTION 4. RESOURCE SUSTAINABILITY STANDARDS

The Court encourages adoption of statewide planning and regulatory standards prioritizing sustainability,
including:

• Integration of water-efficient or closed-loop cooling technologies;
 Utilization of reclaimed, recycled, or non-potable water sources where feasible;
 Demand response participation and load management requirements to protect grid stability.

SECTION 5. LEGISLATIVE ACTION REQUESTED

The Cooke County Commissioners' Court respectfully requests that the Governor and Texas Legislature:

 I . Examine policy options that would improve transparency regarding projected electricity demand and
 water usage of large-scale computing facilities; and
2. Designate this matter as an interim study priority for appropriate legislative committees to evaluate
 grid reliability, water consumption, local authority, infrastructure impacts, and fiscal implications; and
3. Evaluate ways to ensure coordination with local governments when large-scale industrial projects
 may significantly impact local infrastructure, energy services, or natural resources; and
4. Consider whether counties should be granted limited tools to assess infrastructure and resource
 impacts associated with hyperscale industrial developments; and
5. Consider, if circumstances warrant timely action, inclusion of this issue on the call of a Special
 Session of the Texas Legislature to address urgent reliability, water supply, and local governance
 concerns; and
6. Enact legislation providing counties and municipalities meaningful participation and authority in land
 use, infrastructure planning, and resource impact review related to large-scale data center
 developments.

SECTION 6. DISTRIBUTION

Copies of this Resolution shall be transmitted to:
• Greg Abbott, Governor of Texas;
• Dan Patrick, Lieutenant Governor of Texas;
• Phil King, Texas State Senator;
• Helen Kerwin, Texas State Representative;
• The Public Utility Commission ofTexas;
• The Texas Commission on Environmental Quality;
• The Electric Reliability Council of Texas;
• The Texas Water Development Board; and
• Appropriate regional and local governmental bodies.

 nd approved on this, the 11th day of May 2026.
signals.authorityCaveat: This action addresses state policy and does not itself impose a county restriction.

signals.title: Crusoe data-center tax abatement approved
signals.summary: Nolan County approved a tax-abatement agreement for the Crusoe data-center campus.
signals.quote:
PURSUANT TO CHAPTER 312 OF THE TEXAS TAX CODE BETWEEN NOLAN COUNTY (THE ‘COUNTY’)
AND CRUSOE ENERGY SYSTEMS, LLC (OR ITS SUBSIDIARY) (COLLECTIVELY ‘CRUSOE’). IN
COMPLIANCE WITH SECTION 312.207(c) OF THE TEXAS TAX CODE, IN PERTINENT PART, THE
PROPOSED TAX ABATEMENT AGREMENT UNDER CONSIDERATION CONSISTS OF THE FOLLOWING:

a. Crusoe, owners of interests in lands eligible for abatement agreements within the reinvestment zone,
submitted applications for tax abatement agreements.

b. The abatement agreement is proposed within the Crusoe Energy - Nolan County Reinvestment Zone
designated by the commissioners Court of Nolan County, Texas on March 9, 2026, located in central Nolan
County. A map of the existent reinvestment zone is available for inspections in the office of the county
Judge.

c. The proposed improvements are related to the construction of a state-of-the-art data center campus with a
rated capacity of 200MW including the construction of a natural gas power plant including building and
improvements including data center halls and structural foundations, computer hardware, servers, racking
and mounting structures, electrical substations, switchgear, and low voltage transformers, back-up power
generation systems and infrastructure, mechanical cooling and thermal management systems, network
infrastructure and fiber optic connectivity, and security systems, fire control systems and associated site
work.

d. The estimated cost of the improvements shall be in excess of $7,000,000,000. (attachment)

Motion was made by Commissioner White, seconded by Commissioner Mahaffey to approve the Crusoe Energy Systems
Tax Abatement Agreement with the water restriction changes, and gift percent changed to 10%. Vote was taken and
members of the court unanimously voted yes. Motion carried.
signals.authorityCaveat: The water-related terms occur within project facilitation and do not establish a general data-center restriction.
signals.model: ChatGPT Pro extended review + deterministic local verification
signals.projectName: Crusoe Energy Systems data center campus

signals.summary: A public hearing on tax-abatement guidelines included energy-storage facilities among eligible facility types.
signals.quote:
PUBLIC HEARING TAX ABATEMENT GUIDELINES AND CRITERIA 10:00 A.M.:

No public attendance.

CONSIDER _THE GUIDELINES _ AND _ CRITERIA__FOR__GRANTING__TAX
ABATEMENTS, HEAR FROM JEFF SNOWDEN FROM CAPEX RESOURCES GROUP:
GUIDELINES AND CRITERIA FOR GRANTING TAX ABATEMENTS IN

_ REINVESTMENT ZONES

LIPSCOMB COUNTY, TEXAS —

1 PURPOSE

Lipscomb County, hereinafter referred to as “County” is committed to the promotion of quality
development in all parts of the County and to improving the quality of life for its citizens. In order to help
meet these goals, the County will consider evaluating tax Incentives within designated and proposed
reinvestment zones, accepting applications for tax abatement, and entering Into tax abatement
agreements to stimulate growth and development.

It Is the intent of the County that such incentives will be provided in accordance with the
procedures and criteria In this document and In Chapter 312 of the Texas Tax Code. However, nothing In

incentives to oli - All such applica

development, redevelopment, and Improvement specified In the contract will be eligible for abatement.
All abatement contracts will be for a term no longer than allowed by law. Additionally, the Lipscomb
County Commissioners Court reserves the right to negotiate a tax abatement. agreement In order to
compete favorably with other communities.

signals.title: Zapata Wind tax-abatement agreement noticed
signals.summary: The county issued public notice of possible action on a tax-abatement agreement for Zapata Wind.
signals.quote:
The Zapata County Commissioners Court shall hold a regular meeting on April 13th, 2026, at 9
am and the following agenda item will be considered:

 workforce development office in securing assistance for vendors and employment.

 utilized for the purpose of a solar power project will be under long term lease with
 the current landowner. Any existing improvements to land that is leased are

signals.title: Wise County adopts resolution seeking state data-center guardrails
signals.summary: The county adopted a resolution seeking state tools and later promoted it while noting the absence of a pending local project or abatement request.
signals.quote:
10. Consider and act on a resolution regarding the establishment of additional data centers in
the State of Texas:

¢ Clark reassures everyone that the County does not have any pending data center
project at this time.

¢ The Court is preparing for the inevitable by learning more about data centers,
determining what questions should be asked (when approached by businesses), and
consulting with other counties about their experience with data centers and what they
wish they would have done differently.

¢ Clark reminds everyone that the County does not have land zoning authority to reject
the building of a data center (or any other project) unless a tax abatement is requested

or if the project is a sexually-oriented business.

¢ Since the state grants the counties authorities over local-level projects, Clark suggests
requesting “guardrails” and county tools for future data center developments.

MARCH 9, 2026 —- COMMISSIONERS’ COURT Page 5|13

¢ Clark reads the proposed resolution and highlights environmental, water, and (limited
reporting of) grid reliability concerns. Additionally, he points out that the County
bears the primary financial responsibility for county road maintenance and repair and
yet, the County possesses “limited statutorial authority to require proportionate cost
participation from certain large-scale development impacting such structure”
(attachments).

* Clark states copies of the resolution will be distributed to Governor Greg Abbott,
Luitenant Governor Dan Patrick, State Senator Tan Parker, State Representative
Andy Hopper, Public Utility Commission of Texas (PUC), Electric Reliability
Council of Texas (ERCOT), Texas Water Development Board (TWDB), and
appropriate regional and local governmental bodies.

¢ The following people express their concerns about the data center and water usage is
the prominent topic: Amanda Balderas, Jim Harvison, Kimberlee Swoboda, Sheriff
Cary Mellema, Paul and Brenda Platt, Debbie Steele, Charles Clark, Carrie Baran,
and Bethany Jarrell.

¢ Clark and Shawn provide additional information about data centers:

They could haul in their water from anywhere in the state
Two types of data center designs:
© Open loop ~ takes in large amount of water everyday
© Closed loop — up front fill of approximately 200,000 gallons of
water with a daily usage of approximately 1,200 gallons per day
200,000 gallons of water is estimated to last 40 years, which is the life
expectancy of the data center based on Jack County’s abatement contract

vv

Vv

¢ Clark brings up the decommission of data centers after usage

¢ Clark suggests that anyone wishing to support the County’s position should write a
letter/email along with a copy of the resolution and urge the state to take action. The
resolution will be posted on the County’s website under the heading “News Flash”.

¢ Clark states the County will bring in a third party, should an abatement is needed.

¢ Motion made by Burns, seconded by Shawn, and unanimously adopted by the

Court to approve the resolution of the Wise County Commissioners’ Court
regarding the establishment of data centers in the state of Texas.
signals.authorityCaveat: The county stated that it lacks zoning authority to regulate data centers; the resolution is advocacy, not a local restriction.

signals.quote:
ENGIE/ PRAIRIE HILL WIND PROJECT

On this the 17 day of March, 2026, came on for consideration the matter regarding the Engie/ Prairie Hill
Wind Project: Presentation / Update regarding Decommission of Wind Turbines. Mr. Russ Keene with
Engie North America updated the Court regarding the Prairie Hill Wind Project: Decommissioning. After
discussion, Judge Felton made a motion to accept that report for recording purposes and it was seconded
simultaneously by Commissioner Smith and Commissioner Perry. A vote being called for, voting in favor
of said motion was Judge Felton, Commissioner Smith, Commissioner Wilson, Commissioner Jones and
Commissioner Perry. It is ordered by the Court that said authorization be, and the same are hereby, approved

signals.title: Wilson County adopts solar-eligible tax-abatement policy
signals.summary: Wilson County adopted a general tax-abatement policy whose eligibility provisions can cover solar arrays.
signals.quote:
WILSON COUNTY
TAX ABATEMENT POLICY
Preamble

Pursuant to Chapter 312, Texas Tax Code (the “Act”), Wilson County may consider an
application for tax abatement, designate a reinvestment zone, and enter into a tax abatement
agreement as provided for in this Tax Abatement Policy. This Policy and the guidelines and
criteria outlined herein were approved by Resolution No. adopted by the
Wilson County Commissioners Court on March 9, 2026, after a public hearing on March 9, 2026,
and approval of a Resolution providing that the County elects to become eligible in tax abatement
pursuant to the Act.

I. Abatement Policy

A. Investment. To enter into an abatement agreement, the Commissioners Court must find that
the project will result in a significant investment being made in the County. Unless
additional factors are deemed to provide value to the County, the minimum investment for
abatement is as follows:

i. New business: $1,000,000, and
ii. Expansion of existing business: $300,000.

B. Job Creation. Abatement on eligible real and fixed personal property requires new job
creation, or, in the case of expansion, sustained employment levels.

C. Criteria. In determining whether to designate a reinvestment zone the County shall consider
the Criteria provided in Section 312.202, Texas Tax Code, and with regard to whether to
enter into a tax abatement agreement, the Commissioners Court shall consider the
following criteria, among others determined to be appropriate by the Court:

i. Site and Improvements

Existing value of land and existing improvements, if any;

Type and value of proposed improvements;

Productive life of proposed improvements;

Overall compatibility with the zoning ordinances and comprehensive plan,
if any, for the area; and

e. Environmental impacts of project.

Beep

ii. Economic Spinoff
a. Number and dollar amounts of all construction contracts and subcontracts
awarded on the project;
b. Impact on the business opportunities of existing businesses and the
attraction of new business to the area, if any; and
c. Disadvantaged business entity and Wilson County contractors represented
in total construction, suppliers, and services contracts.

Page | of 12

iii. Jobs
a. Number of existing jobs to be retained by proposed improvements, if any;

b. Number and type of new jobs, if any, to be created by proposed
improvements;

c. Diversity of employment base;
d. Local employment opportunities; and
e. Competitive wages and benefits for employees.

iv. Public Costs and Benefits
a. Costs to be incurred by Wilson County, if any, to provide facilities or
services directly resulting from the new improvements;
b. Types and values of public improvements, if any, to be made by applicant
seeking abatement; and
c. Amount of ad valorem property tax to be paid to Wilson County during and
after expiration of the abatement agreement.

. Ad Valorem Taxes. Unless expressly provided, County approval of tax abatement applies
only to County ad valorem taxes and may be restricted to include only County maintenance
and operations taxes, excluding interest and sinking fund taxes. County approval of tax
abatement may also apply to both Hospital District and Emergency Services District ad
valorem taxes, provided that the County is statutorily required to approve the tax rate for
such districts or levies their ad valorem taxes as described in Section 312.004, Texas Tax
Code.

i. Existing Property Value. The value of existing real and personal property currently
on the tax rolls will remain taxable and be included in the base value, even if
personal property is moved to a new, abated location or replaced due to
modernization or expansion. Abatement may be only granted for the additional
value of eligible property improvements made subsequent to and listed in an
abatement agreement between the County and the property owner or lessee, subject
to such limitations as Commissioners Court may require.

. Application Must Precede Commencement of Project. A project is ineligible for abatement
if the application for County abatement is filed after the commencement for construction,
alteration, or installation of new improvements.

. Ineligible Property. The following types of property shall be fully taxable and ineligible
for abatement: inventories; supplies; tools; furnishings, and other forms of movable
personal property (excluding solar array panels and associated equipment); vehicles;
vessels; private aircraft; deferred maintenance investments also, any property included in
the calculation of base year value as defined.

. Compliance with Policy. The County will only enter into abatement agreements that the
County finds meet the guidelines and criteria outlined in this Policy and in the Act.
signals.authorityCaveat: The policy governs discretionary incentives and does not itself approve or restrict a specific solar project.
signals.model: ChatGPT Pro extended review + deterministic local verification

signals.title: Concho officials coordinate rerouting of Lucy Solar water trucks
signals.summary: Paint Rock's mayor raised road-capacity and student-safety concerns about water tankers serving Lucy Solar Farm, and a county commissioner reported asking the project manager to reroute the trucks.
signals.quote:
CONSIDER/DISCUSS/APPROVE PAINT ROCK MAYOR FRANCIS MAUPIN ON TRAFFIC
GOING DOWN SIMS STREET — TAKE APPROPRIATE ACTION

Mayor Maupin stated that there are water tankers turning and going down Sims Street in Paint
Rock delivering water to the Lucy Solar Farm. She stated that there are at least 12 tankers a day
going back and forth. Mayor Maupin said that the city road is not rated to withstand as much
weight as frequently as they are delivering. She said that it is extremely dangerous for them to be
going that route, not only due to the roads, but also due to the students going to and from class

at the school. Commissioner Bradshaw stated that he spoke with the project manager, Mr.
Cooper, this morning about having the trucks re-routed to go down FM 765. Mr. Cooper said that
he would send out the message.
signals.authorityCaveat: This is informational or operational monitoring, not a generally applicable restriction.

signals.title: Van Zandt County order adopts temporary solar moratorium
signals.summary: The quoted county order declares a temporary moratorium on solar-project surface and construction operations.
signals.quote:
VAN ZANDT COUNTY, TEXAS
GREEN ENERGY MORATORIUM

AN ORDER OF VAN ZANDT COUNTY, TEXAS, DECLARING A TEMPORARY
MORATORIUM ON SURFACE AND CONSTRUCTION OPERATIONS RELATED TO THE
COMMENCEMENT, BUILDING AND/OR COMPLETION OF ALL GREEN ENERGY
PROJECTS WITHIN THE BORDERS OF VAN ZANDT COUNTY, INCLUDING BUT NOT
LIMITED TO BATTERY ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS, SOLAR FACILITIES, AND
WIND ENERGY PROJECTS; MAKING WRITTEN FINDINGS OF NECESSITY PURSUANT
TO TEXAS HEALTH AND SAFETY CODE § 121.003; PROVIDING FOR NOTICE AND
PUBLIC HEARING; PROVIDING FOR SEVERABILITY; AND ESTABLISHING AN
EFFECTIVE DATE.

PURPOSE AND SCOPE OF STUDY DURING MORATORIUM

The purpose of this moratorium is to allow Van Zandt County sufficient time to study, review,
and, if necessary, evaluate compliance with health, fire and safety requirements, cybersecurity
safeguards, and enforcement mechanisms applicable to all green energy projects.

During the moratorium period, the County shall evaluate:

1. Risks to public health, safety, and welfare arising from green energy projects, including fire
suppression, emergency response capacity, and proximity to residences.

2. Cybersecurity and operational technology risks associated with foreign-manufactured
hardware, software, firmware, and communications systems which have been included on the
Governor of Texas prohibited company list.

3. Compliance with current and anticipated State of Texas directives, including executive actions
of the Governor addressing prohibited technologies, critical infrastructure protection, and foreign
adversary influence (SEE Schedules ATTACHED).

4. Adequacy of existing County ordinances in compliance with State Law, including but not
limited to NFPA fire code and the Lone Star Infrastructure Protection Act, and the Critical
Infrastructure Protection Act, to regulate siting, construction, operation, monitoring, and
decommissioning of green energy facilities while protecting the infrastructure in accord with said
the referenced state laws.

5. The immediate necessity is because the County has received plans from one developer
who expressly intends to use equipment from a manufacturer which has been placed on the
Governor’s prohibited entity list. The County is responsible for the health, safety and
welfare of the citizens, therefore, further research and study is required to insure the health,
safety and welfare of Van Zandt County.

NOTICE IS HEREBY GIVEN that the Commissioners Court of Van Zandt County, Texas, will
conduct a public hearing pursuant to Texas Health and Safety Code § 121.003 on the proposed
Green Energy Moratorium Ordinance.

DATE: February 11, 2026
TIME: 9AM
LOCATION: Van Zandt County Courthouse, County Court at Law Courtroom

All interested persons are invited to appear and be heard. Written comments may be submitted
prior to the hearing.

FINDINGS OF FACT - EXHIBIT A (PUBLIC SAFETY AND
INFRASTRUCTURE)

1. Green energy projects increasingly rely on complex, interconnected operational technology and
communications systems integral to critical infrastructure.

2. Failures or compromises of such systems pose material risks to public safety, emergency
response, and property.

3. Rural counties such as Van Zandt County face heightened risks due to limited emergency
response resources.

FINDINGS OF FACT - EXHIBIT B (CYBERSECURITY AND FOREIGN
INFLUENCE)

executive actions identifying and restricting prohibited technologies associated with foreign
adversaries.

Prohibited Technologies List due to cybersecurity and national security concerns.

3. Green energy projects may incorporate foreign-linked software, firmware, and communications

EFFECTIVE DATE.

limited to NFPA fire code and the Lone Star Infrastructure Protection Act, and the Critical

signals.title: Affidavits request halt to solar projects
signals.summary: Resident or stakeholder affidavits ask the county to halt planned solar projects pending investigations and compliance review.
signals.quote:
3. | make this affidavit in support of a request by residents and stakeholders in Van Zandt
County, Texas, for a moratorium on all planned solar, wind, and battery energy storage
system (BESS) builds within the county until the ongoing investigation by the Texas
Attorney General is completed and until current projects, including but not limited to the
Amador Energy Storage project, demonstrate full compliance with National Fire
Protection Association (NFPA) standards, state fire codes, control system cybersecurity
requirements, and other safety and security mandates.
signals.authorityCaveat: The affidavits are public requests and do not themselves establish county action.

signals.title: Hudspeth County adopts resolution seeking state data-center safeguards
signals.summary: Hudspeth County adopted a resolution supporting responsibly sited data centers while opposing high-water cooling and inadequately safeguarded projects, and requesting state study, standards, and greater local authority.
signals.quote:
SECTION I. POSITION OF THE COURT

The Hudspeth County Commissioners Court supports responsible economic development, including
appropriately designed and sited data center facilities. However, the Court formally expresses opposition
to the use of open-loop evaporative cooling systems or other high-volume potable water consumption
technologies in large-scale, high continuous-load data center facilities within water-constrained regions,
absent demonstrated sustainable supply and mitigation safeguards. The Court further opposes any data

center development that fails to incorporate adequate safeguards to protect county resources and
residents.

The Court further states that support for additional data center development in the State of Texas should
be conditioned upon the implementation of comprehensive, enforceable safeguards to:

¢ Protect electric grid reliability and prevent cost burdens on residential, agricultural, and small-
business ratepayers;

e Ensure responsible, sustainable, and transparent water use practices;

¢ Protect agricultural lands, rangelands, wildlife habitats, and natural drainage systems by
requiring careful site selection, avoidance of prime farmland and ecologically sensitive areas
where practicable, and appropriate mitigation of habitat fragmentation and stormwater impacts;

¢ Prevent degradation of county roads, drainage systems, and other public infrastructure; and

e Safeguard community welfare, public health, and environmental quality.

SECTION II. TRANSPARENCY AND REPORTING

The Court urges the State of Texas and relevant regulatory agencies to require full and transparent public
reporting of anticipated electricity demand, water usage, and infrastructure impacts for all proposed data
center developments prior to final approval.

SECTION ILL. INDEPENDENT IMPACT ANALYSIS
No future data center project should proceed without a rigorous, independent assessment of:

e Impacts on ERCOT grid reliability and transmission capacity;

e Impacts on regional water availability and drought contingency planning;

e Impacts on agricultural lands, rangelands, wildlife habitats, natural drainage patterns, and
potential habitat fragmentation, including effects on prime farmland, soil integrity, and
stormwater runoff;

e Long-term infrastructure costs borne by taxpayers; and

e Effects on surrounding residential, agricultural, and commercial land uses.

SECTION IV. RESOURCE SUSTAINABILITY STANDARDS

The Court encourages adoption of statewide planning and regulatory standards prioritizing
sustainability, including:

e Integration of water-efficient or closed-loop cooling technologies;
e Utilization of reclaimed, recycled, or non-potable water sources where feasible;
e Demand response participation and load management requirements to protect grid stability.

SECTION V. LEGISLATIVE ACTION REQUESTED
The Hudspeth County Commissioners Court respectfully requests that the Governor and Texas
Legislature:

1. Designate this matter as an interim study priority for appropriate legislative committees to
evaluate grid reliability, water consumption, local authority, infrastructure impacts, and fiscal
implications; and

2. Consider, if circumstances warrant timely action, inclusion of this issue on the call of a Special
Session of the Texas Legislature to address urgent reliability, water supply, and local
governance concerns; and

3. Enact legislation providing counties and municipalities meaningful participation and authority
in land use, infrastructure planning, and resource impact review related to large-scale data
center developments.

SECTION VI. DISTRIBUTION
Copies of this Resolution shall be transmitted to:

Greg Abbott, Governor of Texas;

Dan Patrick, Lieutenant Governor of Texas;

Tan Parker, Texas State Senator;

Andy Hopper, Texas State Representative;

The Public Utility Commission of Texas;

The Electric Reliability Council of Texas;

The Texas Water Development Board; and
Appropriate regional and local governmental bodies.

cece ee eee

PASSED AND APPROVED UNANIMOUSLY BY THE HUDSPETH COUNTY
COMMISSIONERS COURT ON-THIS 14! DAY OF APRIL, 2026.
signals.authorityCaveat: This is a county resolution advocating state action and safeguards; it does not itself establish a local data-center moratorium or enforceable siting code.
signals.model: ChatGPT Pro extended review + deterministic local verification

signals.quote:

 Excerpts from the letter from Van Zandt County Fire Marshal Kevin Palmer concerning the
 denial of all documents pertaining to the Amador BESS Project located in Van Zandt County,
 TX.

 “After reviewing the documents formally transmitted to my office on 10 December 2025, I am
 formally rejecting all documents associated with this project.

 In May of 2025, stakeholders agreed that all documents pertaining to this project would be
 approved by the Fire Protection Engineer (FPE) HILLER prior to being sent to my office for

 NFPA 69, Emergency Response Plan (ERP), and Fire Risk Assessment (FRA). Therefore, all
 future documents that are not approved by Hiller shall be rejected. Additionally, all comments

signals.title: Blue Heron Solar tax abatement denied
signals.summary: Mills County negotiated tax-abatement and possible road-use terms for Blue Heron Solar, then voted not to provide the requested tax abatement.
signals.quote:
3 Consider and possible action on Blue Heron Solar Project tax abatement agreement pursuant to
Chapter 312 of the Texas Tax Code between Mills County (the “County”) and Blue Heron Solar, LLC
(or its subsidiary) (collectively “Blue Heron”) In compliance with Section 312 207 © of the Texas Tax
Code, in pertinent part, the proposed tax abatement agreement under consideration consists of the
following

1 Bule Heron Solar, LLC, owners of interests in lands eligible for abatement agreement
within the remvestment zone submutted applications for tax abatement agreements

2 The abatement agreement 1s proposed within the Blue Heron Solar — Mills County
Reinvestment Zone designated by the Commissioners Court of Mills County, Texas on November 24,
2025, located in western Mills County Map of the existent remvestment zone 1s available for
inspection 1n the office of the County Judge

3 The proposed improvements are related to the construction of solar energy facility
including solar panels, tracking systems, battery energy storage systems (as such term 1s used in
common language), inverters, transformers, foundations, containers, cabling, collection lines, collections
stations, transmission lines, substations, interconnection facilities, operation and maintenance facilities,
and other infrastructure, materials, and equipment customary and necessary for the operation of a solar
power electric generation

4 The estimated cost of the improvements shall be m excess of $267,000,000 00

Discussion amongst attomeys and Commissioners regarding the 35% abatement Commussioner
Willams noted that he has not seen the agreement from Mr Allen This agreement was sent to the
Judge only Mr Allen stated that if the commissioners agreed to the 35%, the agreement would be sent

to all Commissioner Williams would like to see the agreement prior to voting 20% 1s not an option for
abatement as Blue Heron would not consider lower than 35% Mr Allen also stated that the Court could
vote on what they want (10% or 20% abatement) and then present it to Blue Heron Judge Johnson made
a motion to not provide any abatement for Blue Heron Commissioner Head provided the second

Judge Johnson, Commissioner Head and Commissioner Sullivan voted for the motion to NOT provide
any abatement for Blue Heron Motion carried 3-2 Commissioner Partin and Williams voted against the
motion
signals.authorityCaveat: Denial of a discretionary tax incentive does not prohibit or regulate the underlying solar project.
